# ── KEY CONTENT ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
key_claim: "There's a difference between not dying and being alive. You can be functioning, fed, employed, and fine on paper and still be in the group that merely \"didn't die\" — going through the motions with the curtains drawn. The work isn't to survive harder; it's to notice you've been surviving, and to come back to life."

content_summary: "Perel, who grew up among Holocaust survivors in Antwerp, describes noticing two kinds of survivors in her community: one group that \"didn't die,\" and one group that \"came back to life.\" The first had survived in the literal sense but stayed shuttered — plastic on the couches, curtains drawn, going through the motions of a life. The second had also survived everything, yet had somehow re-entered aliveness. Her distinction reframes the engagement-drought experience precisely: the question isn't whether you're functioning — many people function while feeling dead — it's whether you've come back to life. Surviving and being alive are not the same thing, and noticing the gap is the first move back."

attribution_text: "Esther Perel, The Tim Ferriss Show, \"Esther Perel (#241)\" (2018)"
# NOTE: Human must verify completeness — check speaker name, show, episode title, date.

user_problem_match_notes: "Engagement-drought / numbness where the user is functioning but feels dead inside. Perel's survival-vs-revival reframe names that precisely without pathologizing it. Distinct from Huberman (dopamine mechanism), Brown (numbing as avoidance), and Achor (happiness advantage) — Perel's lever is the surviving-vs-alive distinction itself. NOTE: in the source she extends this toward desire/eroticism; this SIO uses only the (non-relational) survivor-community observation, which generalizes to engagement broadly."

resonance_match_notes: "Intellectual/measured (reflective), moderate, delivered as a personal-historical story. Fills the thin engagement-drought intellectual/measured and story cells. There was one group that didn't die, and one group that came back to life.
